coredata.dc:title: Green and sustainable synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from crown daisy for information encryption application
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Jing
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a sustainable and eco-friendly synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) from crown daisy, a common vegetable, and o-phenylenediamine via a hydrothermal method. The synthesized G-CDs were comprehensively characterized using various spectroscopic techniques, revealing a maximum excitation wavelength of 436 nm and emission at 539 nm with a quantum yield of up to 14.5 % in ethanol. The optical properties was systematically investigated, demonstrating the excellent salt tolerance, pH stability, and photostability of the G-CDs. Notably, the G-CDs exhibited excitation-independent emissions behavior, indicating a single type of fluorescent emission center. The practical application of G-CDs as anti-counterfeiting ink was demonstrated by generating a clear QR code pattern under UV illumination, showcasing their potential for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. This work not only provides a sustainable and cost-effective approach for G-CD synthesis but also highlights their promising prospects in optical anti-counterfeiting and information encryption domains.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Crown daisy
Green carbon dots
Quantum yield
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the G-CDs synthesis.

Effect of precursors. Comparison of (a) fluorescence spectra, (b) fluorescence intensity, (c) fluorescence lifetime, (d) fluorescence intensity and absorbance of carbon dots synthesized from Crown Daisy and different nitrogen sources.

Structural Characterization of G-CDs. (a) TEM image. The inset is the particle size distribution; (b) HRTEM image; (c) FT-IR spectrum; (d) XRD spectrum; (e) XPS survey spectrum; (f-h) C1s, N1s and O1s fine spectra.

Optical Characteristics of G-CDs. (a) Three-dimensional plot of the excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs; (b) Two-dimensional projection; (c) Excitation spectrum (blue), and emission spectrum (multicolor); (d) The UV absorption spectra of G-CDs at different pH; (e) Emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths; (f) Normalized emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths.
Optical Stability of G-CDs. Effects of (a) pH, (b) temperature, (c) NaCl concentration, and (d) time on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs.
Effect of solvent. (a) Fluorescence spectra and (b) quantum yields of G-CDs in different solvents.

G-CDs as Encrypted Fluorescent Anti-counterfeiting Ink. (a) Photos of two-dimensional code under sunlight and ultraviolet lamp respectively; (b) Photos of two-dimensional code under continuous irradiation of ultraviolet lamp; (c) Photos of G-CDs in cuvettes under sunlight and ultraviolet light, respectively; (d) Photos of G-CDs on glass slides under sunlight and ultraviolet light, respectively;(e) Photos of glass slides under continuous irradiation of ultraviolet lamp.
Comparison of quantum yields of other carbon dots and G-CDs in this work.

head.ce:title: Green and sustainable synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from crown daisy for information encryption application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a sustainable and eco-friendly synthesis of green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) from crown daisy, a common vegetable, and o-phenylenediamine via a hydrothermal method. The synthesized G-CDs were comprehensively characterized using various spectroscopic techniques, revealing a maximum excitation wavelength of 436 nm and emission at 539 nm with a quantum yield of up to 14.5 % in ethanol. The optical properties was systematically investigated, demonstrating the excellent salt tolerance, pH stability, and photostability of the G-CDs. Notably, the G-CDs exhibited excitation-independent emissions behavior, indicating a single type of fluorescent emission center. The practical application of G-CDs as anti-counterfeiting ink was demonstrated by generating a clear QR code pattern under UV illumination, showcasing their potential for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications. This work not only provides a sustainable and cost-effective approach for G-CD synthesis but also highlights their promising prospects in optical anti-counterfeiting and information encryption domains.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have garnered substantial interest as a novel photoluminescent nanomaterial owing to their exceptional photoluminescence performance, low toxicity [1], high biocompatibility [2], and potential applications in chemical sensing [3], bioimaging [4], drug delivery [5], photocatalysis [6,7], and anti-counterfeiting [18–20]. Recently, researchers have explored various synthetic routes for CDs. Bai et al. produced polar fluorescent CDs via a hydrothermal method using 2,3-dichloro-5,6-dicyanoquinone and resorcinol [8]. Zhai et al. successfully synthesized N-doped carbon fluorescent quantum dots through a facile hydrothermal approach [9]. Sharmila et al. utilized a hydrothermal technique to incorporate CDs with folic acid, hydroxyapatite, and silica for bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting applications [10]. While these studies have laid a solid foundation for anti-counterfeiting applications of CDs, challenges such as high pollution, high cost, and operational complexity persist. CDs synthesized from natural precursors are abundant, non-toxic, environmentally friendly, and renewable, making them a promising avenue for cost-effective, eco-friendly, and reliable fluorescent materials [11].

Glebionis coronaria (L.) Cass. ex Spach, commonly known as crown daisy, is an annual or biennial herbaceous plant belonging to the Asteraceae family [12]. It is a spring vegetable in southern China. Originally from the Mediterranean region, crown daisy has been cultivated in China since before the Tang Dynasty, with a long history of cultivation. Additionally, crown daisy is rich in vitamins and various amino acids, earning it the name ‘Emperor's Vegetable’. Compared to other carbon sources, crown daisy is a low-cost option for CD preparation, making it an ideal choice. Therefore, to reduce pollution, lower costs, and simplify the preparation process while achieving good fluorescence conversion efficiency, the common, sustainable, and low-cost crown daisy and o-phenylenediamine were selected as precursors to synthesize green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) via a hydrothermal method. The synthesized G-CDs were characterized for their structure, surface composition, and optical properties. The quantum yields of the CDs in different solvents were investigated, demonstrating their good salt tolerance and photostability. Finally, the practical application of G-CDs is introduced using anti-counterfeiting ink as an example, successfully applied in QR code anti-counterfeiting. These results indicate that G-CDs can achieve unique optical properties in environmentally friendly ink formulations, showing prospects for safe and eco-friendly applications in the anti-counterfeiting field, with potential for use in anti-counterfeiting printing.

Crown daisy was purchased from a supermarket in Chengdu. O-phenylenediamine (OPD, analytical grade) and quinine sulfate (analytical grade) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Ethanol (analytical grade) was obtained from Chengdu Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd. The water used in the experiments was self-prepared double-distilled water. The hydrothermal reactor (25 mL) was purchased from Xi Niu Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaner was sourced from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd., and the electric thermostatic blast drying oven was obtained from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd.

The G-CDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method, and the specific synthesis steps are detailed as follows. 30 g of the stems and leaves of crown daisy along with 180 mL of ethanol was ground in a kitchen blender, followed by a 12-hours soaking for sufficient extraction. Afterwards, the extraction liquid was then centrifuged, and 5 mL of the centrifuged liquid from the crown daisy was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene liner. Subsequently, 0.6 g of o-phenylenediamine was added, along with 5 mL of water as the solvent. After stirring, the liner was placed in an ultrasonic machine for 10 min of sonication. Then the liner was transferred to a hydrothermal reactor and heated in an oven set at 180 °C for a 5-hours reaction, resulting in the formation of G-CDs solution. Further use of silica gel column chromatography can produce purified G-CDs solution. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV-visible spectrophotometer with a scanning wavelength range of 200–700 nm. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using a VERIEX 70 FTIR spectrometer. The G-CDs powder was mixed with KBr, pressed into pellets, and scanned in the range of 400–4000 cm-1. Fluorescence spectra were acquired on a Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a wavelength range of 300–800 nm and a slit width of 10 nm. The UV lamp was supplied by Fengling with the model MT810 (365 nm 40 W). XRD was measured on a miniflex600 desktop X-ray diffractometer with an angle of 10°−90°. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher).

The as-prepared G-CDs solution was diluted with ethanol in a 1:1 ratio to serve as a fluorescent ink. A stamp engraved with a QR code was directly inked with the diluted G-CDs solution and immediately stamped onto a blank paper. The paper with the stamped QR code was then photographed under both daylight and UV (365 nm) illumination.

To investigate the influence of precursors on the properties of G-CDs, this study explored the selection of nitrogen sources. Using Crown Daisy as the carbon source, various nitrogen sources were employed as doping agents to synthesize carbon dots, including triethylamine, ethylenediamine, hydroxylamine hydrochloride, and o-phenylenediamine. As shown in Figs. 2a and 2b, G-CDs synthesized from Crown Daisy and o-phenylenediamine exhibit the optimal fluorescence intensity. To validate this hypothesis, further measurements of fluorescence lifetime and quantum yield were conducted for carbon dots synthesized from different precursors. Fig. 2c presents the fluorescence lifetime data for the carbon dots from various sources, revealing lifetimes of 1.8 ns, 2.1 ns, 1.9 ns, 2.5 ns, and 4.5 ns. There is a correlation between fluorescence lifetime and intensity, suggesting that an increase in fluorescence lifetime may lead to enhanced fluorescence intensity. Figs. 2d and 2e illustrate the quantum yields of carbon dots from different precursors. It is evident that G-CDs synthesized from Crown Daisy and o-phenylenediamine exhibit the highest quantum yield, further confirming their ideal fluorescence performance. These results indicate that o-phenylenediamine can significantly enhance the luminescence intensity of G-CDs, optimize their fluorescence properties, and increase the emission wavelength. This enhancement may be attributed to the conjugated system of the phenyl ring structure in o-phenylenediamine, which forms stronger electronic interactions with the surface of the carbon dots, thus effectively modulating their electronic structure and energy levels, leading to significant improvements in fluorescence properties. Furthermore, this conjugated system can form conjugation with the surface of the carbon dots during high-temperature synthesis, extending the conjugation length. An increase in conjugation length can narrow the energy gap, resulting in a red shift of the fluorescence emission peak. In conclusion, the G-CDs we synthesized exhibit ideal fluorescence properties.

We employed various analytical techniques to characterize the structure of G-CDs. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image, as shown in Fig. 3a , revealed that G-CDs exhibit a particulate distribution with an average size of approximately 4 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 3b demonstrates the presence of lattice fringes in G-CDs with a spacing of about 0.21 nm, which is characteristic of the typical graphene lattice. To elucidate the compositional details of the surface functional groups on G-CDs, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was employed. The results, depicted in Fig. 3c, reveal an absorption peak in the range of 3400–3500 cm-1, attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH or OH [13]; a peak at 2364 cm-1, assigned to the stretching vibrations of CN [14]; a peak around 1600 cm-1 should belong to the vibration absorption peak of C = N, and a peak at 1404 cm-1, corresponding to the C=O stretching vibrations [15]. The X-ray diffraction pattern of G-CDs is shown in Fig. 3d. A broad peak appears at 22.8 °, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of the bulk graphite. This may be due to the small size and controllable carbonization of carbon dots, which indicates the presence of graphene or graphite-like features. These findings indicate successful nitrogen doping in the synthesized G-CDs, suggesting the presence of water-soluble functional groups such as -NH2 and -OH. The elemental composition and valence states of G-CDs were further analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum XPS of the prepared sample is presented in Fig. 3e, revealing the presence of C, N, and O with respective contents of 65.48 %, 8.61 %, and 25.91 %. The C1 s fine spectrum of G-CDs, shown in Fig. 3f, can be deconvoluted into three characteristic peaks corresponding to CC/C=C (284.59 eV), CN (285.64 eV), and C=O (288.21 eV) [16]. As illustrated in Fig. 3 g, the N1 s fine spectrum can be divided into three peaks, associated with CN (398.75 eV), NH (400.17 eV), and C=N (407.11 eV) [17,18]. As shown in Fig. 3h, the fine map of O1 s can be divided into two peaks, corresponding to C=O (531.29 eV) and OH (532.66 eV) [19]. Both FT-IR and XPS results collectively confirm the rich presence of nitrogen and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the synthesized G-CDs, which may be related to the redox reaction during the synthesis of G-CDs.

The optical characteristics of the G-CDs were elucidated employing ultraviolet-visible absorption spectroscopy, excitation, and emission spectral analysis. Fig. 4a illustrates a three-dimensional plot of the excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs, revealing two distinct excitation peaks. A two-dimensional projection of the excitation-emission matrix of G-CDs is presented in Fig. 4b, clearly delineating the excitation region primarily between 380 nm–480 nm and the emission region predominantly within 500 nm–640 nm. Fig. 4c displays the UV–Vis absorption spectrum along with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum exhibits characteristic bands at around 240 nm and 300 nm, which can be attributed to the typical π-π* transitions of the carbon core and n-π* transitions of the surface functional groups on the carbon dots, respectively [20,21]. It can be inferred that the maximum excitation peak of G-CDs is located at 436 nm, with a maximum emission wavelength of 539 nm. Subsequently, by color-filling the emission wavelength based on the wavelength, it is observable that the color filled under the emission spectrum curve corresponds to the fluorescence color at the emission wavelength, which is green. Additionally, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the G-CDs emission spectrum is 88 nm. Fig. 4d shows the UV–vis absorption spectra of the G-CDs solution at different pH values. It can be observed that under strongly acidic conditions (pH=1, 2), the absorption bands of G-CDs exhibit a blue shift. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 5a , this blue shift is accompanied by a decrease in fluorescence intensity. This phenomenon can be attributed to the changes in π-π* electronic transitions and the replenishment or depletion of n-π* levels in G-CDs induced by the high concentrations of H+ and OH-. These results indicate that the pH conditions can significantly alter the nature of the energy levels, thereby affecting the types of electronic transitions involved in G-CDs [22,23]. Fig. 4e presents the emission spectra of G-CDs under excitation wavelengths ranging from 410 nm to 460 nm. It is evident that the emission wavelengths of G-CDs remain relatively constant under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 4f, after normalizing these emission spectra, the emission wavelengths are almost overlapping. This phenomenon demonstrates that G-CDs may be surface state carbon dots with a single type of fluorescence emission center, which is related to surface defects or functional groups. Its fluorescence emission mainly comes from a specific electronic transition, rather than a variety of different transitions, so it is insensitive to the excitation wavelength.

To elucidate the physicochemical stability of G-CDs, the effects of reaction system parameters such as pH, temperature, duration, and salt concentration on fluorescence intensity were investigated. Measurements were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 436 nm and an emission wavelength of 539 nm. As shown in Fig. 5a, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs initially increases with pH and then decreases, exhibiting quenching at pH values below 3 and above 11. This pH effect can be attributed to changes in surface charge due to deprotonation. As depicted in Fig. S1–3, we measured the zeta potential at pH 1, 3, and 12, which were -9.3 mV, -2.5 mV, and -21.8 mV, respectively. This indicates that at pH values below 3 and above 11, the functional groups on the G-CDs' surface (such as carboxyl and amine groups) undergo protonation or deprotonation, which affects the charge state of the carbon dots. This change in charge state may lead to potential disruption of the carbon dots' surface structure or degradation of internal fluorescent groups, thereby reducing fluorescence efficiency. Next, we examined the temperature stability of G-CDs over a range from 30 °C to 80 °C. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence intensity of the G-CDs solution showed minimal changes, remaining nearly constant as the temperature gradually increased. Sodium chloride (NaCl) is one of the major inorganic salts in biological systems, and the stability of G-CDs in NaCl solution suggests their potential applications in biological contexts [24]. Therefore, we further investigated the salt tolerance of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 5c, the salt tolerance of G-CDs was assessed by maintaining a consistent concentration of G-CDs while varying the NaCl concentration from 0 to 500 mM. The fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots was not significantly affected by the NaCl concentration, maintaining stable luminescence even in high-salinity environments. To evaluate the photostability, the G-CDs solution was continuously irradiated under a UV lamp. The variation in fluorescence intensity over time is illustrated in Fig. 5d. From the data, it can be observed that the fluorescence intensity remained relatively stable over a 30-days testing period, showing no significant decrease or increase. This indicates that the carbon dots possess good photostability during this timeframe. Therefore, G-CDs demonstrate excellent salt tolerance and stability. On the other hand, careful control of the solution's pH is essential when studying and applying the fluorescent properties of carbon dots to achieve optimal fluorescence outcomes.

To investigate the luminescent properties of G-CDs in different solvents, the fluorescence spectra of G-CDs were measured in water (WAT), acetonitrile (ACN), ethanol (ETH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), acetone (ACE), and tetrahydrofuran (THF), as shown in Fig. 6a . It can be observed that G-CDs exhibit significant fluorescence emission peaks in all solvents, with variations in peak intensity and position across different solvents. The maximum emission intensity occurs in ethanol (ETH), while slightly lower emission intensities are noted in solvents such as acetone and tetrahydrofuran, with the lowest intensity observed in water. To further quantify the impact of solvents on G-CDs, the quantum yields of G-CDs in various solvents were measured using quinine sulfate (REF) as a reference, as shown in Fig. 6b. It is evident that the quantum yield of G-CDs in ethanol (ETH) is the highest, reaching 14.5 %, while lower quantum yields are recorded in other solvents, specifically DMF (14.4 %), acetone (12.7 %), tetrahydrofuran (11.3 %), acetonitrile (11.5 %), and water (7.4 %). This indicates that G-CDs exhibit higher luminescent efficiency in ethanol, which may be attributed to the influence of the solvent's polarity and molecular structure on the luminescent centers of G-CDs. The moderate polarity, hydrogen bonding, and stabilizing effect on the surface states of G-CDs in ethanol likely contribute to its superior luminescent efficiency [24]. This effect is also observed in other solvents with similar characteristics, such as DMF, but is less pronounced in solvents with either excessively high or low polarity. Table 1 presents a comparison of the quantum yields of fluorescent carbon dots reported in the literature with the G-CDs synthesized in this work. It can be observed that most fluorescent carbon dots exhibit relatively low quantum yields. In contrast, the G-CDs synthesized in this study achieve a quantum yield of 14.5 % in ethanol, surpassing the majority of fluorescent carbon dots reported in the literature and demonstrating superior luminescent efficiency.

Carbon dots have found extensive applications across a wide range of fields. In this context, we use anti-counterfeiting as an illustrative example to briefly introduce the application of G-CDs. Anti-counterfeiting measures play a crucial role in various industries. For years, researchers have been in search of unique, highly fluorescent, universal, easily accessible, and environmentally friendly anti-counterfeiting materials [34,35]. The excellent fluorescence intensity and photostability of G-CDs position them as potential alternatives to existing fluorescent inks. A stamp engraved with a QR code was treated with an aqueous solution of G-CDs and subsequently imprinted onto blank paper, as shown in Fig. 7a . Under daylight, the QR code is barely visible; however, under 365 nm UV light, it reveals a bright green pattern with a distinct QR code image. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the QR code remains clearly visible even after continuous exposure to UV radiation for 60 min. We further changed the substrate to glass (microscope slide) to test the stability of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 7d, G-CDs still exhibit strong fluorescence intensity on the slide. Fig. 7e demonstrates that the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs on glass is comparable to that on paper, with only minimal change even after continuous UV exposure for 60 min. These results indicate that G-CDs exhibit remarkable photostability and can be effectively utilized as anti-counterfeiting inks.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized green fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) using Crown Daisy as the carbon source through a hydrothermal method. The structural and optical properties of the synthesized G-CDs were characterized and studied using infrared absorption spectroscopy, UV-visible absorption spectroscopy, molecular fluorescence spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy among other techniques. The results revealed that the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs are 436 nm and 539 nm, respectively. Subsequently, it was observed that G-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission behavior, indicating the presence of a single type of fluorescent emission center. Optimal luminescent conditions for G-CDs were also explored, demonstrating that the synthesized G-CDs exhibit excellent salt tolerance and stability, with a maximum quantum yield of 14.5 % in ethanol. Finally, an aqueous solution of G-CDs was successfully applied as anti-counterfeiting ink on paper, emitting bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Notably, the QR code pattern remained clearly visible even after prolonged UV exposure, successfully achieving information encryption and anti-counterfeiting. This work not only makes the preparation of G-CDs more efficient and environmentally friendly but also provides new insights into their applications in optical anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
Jing Hu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shaogui Wu: Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2024.140794.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: mysterious past, vibrant present, and expansive future
